---
title: "Activity 2"
author: "Varun Gullanki, Amal Parekh"
format: pdf
editor: visual
---

## Nittany Lion Insurance Dashboard

Nittany Valley Insurance Company has requested the development of a predictive model to estimate the total charges billed to a health insurance policy. The objective of this analysis is to determine how demographic, behavioral, and regional factors influence policy costs and to construct a model capable of predicting charges for new policyholders.

The dataset includes information on age, sex, body mass index (BMI), number of dependents, smoking status, geographic region, month of claim, prior claims history, and total charges. Because total charges represent a continuous outcome variable, linear regression methods were selected as an appropriate modeling framework.

Preliminary exploration revealed that insurance charges are highly right-skewed, suggesting the presence of extreme high-cost cases. To address this skewness and better satisfy linear regression assumptions, we considered modeling the logarithm of charges rather than raw charges. This transformation allows for more stable variance and enables interpretation of coefficients in terms of percentage changes in expected costs.

The goal of this report is to explore the data, construct at least two candidate regression models, evaluate their performance, and provide a final recommendation to the company regarding which model should be used for cost prediction.

## Data Description

The dataset contains individual insurance policy records. Each observation represents one policyholder and includes variables such as age, sex, body mass index (BMI), number of children, smoking status, region, month of claim, prior claims, and total charges. The main outcome of interest is total insurance charges.

Summary statistics show that charges vary widely across individuals. A histogram of raw charges shows that the distribution is heavily right-skewed, meaning most individuals have moderate costs while a small number have very high costs.

Because linear regression works best when the response variable is more normally distributed and has stable variance, we considered transforming the outcome. Taking the natural logarithm of charges reduces the skewness and produces a more symmetric distribution. This makes the data better suited for linear regression and allows coefficients to be interpreted as approximate percentage changes in expected charges.

Exploratory plots also show that smokers tend to have much higher charges than non-smokers. In addition, higher BMI appears to be associated with higher costs. These patterns suggest that smoking status and BMI will likely be important predictors in the regression models.

Based on this exploration, we proceed with modeling log-transformed charges using linear regression.

## Modeling Methods/Strategies

To predict insurance charges, we used linear regression with log-transformed charges as the response variable. The log transformation was chosen based on the skewness observed in the exploratory analysis and to better satisfy regression assumptions.

**Model 1** is a baseline additive model that predicts log(charges) using all available predictors: age, sex, BMI, number of children, smoking status, region, month of claim, and prior claims. This model assumes that each predictor has an independent effect on charges.

**Model 2** extends Model 1 by including an interaction between smoking status and BMI. This interaction allows the effect of BMI on charges to differ between smokers and non-smokers. Based on exploratory analysis, smokers tend to have substantially higher costs, and it is reasonable to expect that higher BMI may have a stronger impact among smokers. Including this interaction allows the model to capture that possibility.

Both models were fit using ordinary least squares regression. After fitting each model, we compared their performance using adjusted R-squared values and diagnostic plots to determine whether the interaction term meaningfully improved model fit.

## Results

### A. Initial Results

Model 1 produced an adjusted R-squared of approximately 0.755, indicating that the predictors explain about 75.5% of the variation in log-transformed charges.

Model 2, which includes the interaction between smoking status and BMI, produced an adjusted R-squared of approximately 0.772. This represents an improvement in explanatory power compared to Model 1.

The increase in adjusted R-squared suggests that allowing BMI to have a different effect for smokers improves model fit. In addition, the interaction term in Model 2 was statistically significant, indicating that the relationship between BMI and charges differs by smoking status.

Overall, Model 2 demonstrates a modest but meaningful improvement over Model 1 in explaining variation in insurance charges.

### B. Interpretations

The regression results indicate that several predictors have meaningful effects on insurance charges.

Age has a positive and statistically significant coefficient, indicating that older individuals tend to have higher expected insurance costs, holding other variables constant. Because the model is fit on log-transformed charges, this effect can be interpreted as a percentage increase in expected charges for each additional year of age.

Smoking status is one of the strongest predictors in the model. Smokers have substantially higher expected insurance charges compared to non-smokers, even after controlling for age, BMI, prior claims, and other factors. This confirms the large cost difference observed in the exploratory analysis.

BMI also has a positive association with charges. Higher BMI values are associated with higher expected insurance costs. In Model 2, the interaction between smoking status and BMI is statistically significant. This suggests that the effect of BMI on charges is stronger for smokers than for non-smokers. In other words, increases in BMI lead to larger increases in expected charges among smokers.

Prior claims are also positively associated with higher charges, which is consistent with the expectation that individuals with more prior claims tend to incur higher future costs.

Overall, the results suggest that smoking status, BMI, age, and prior claims are key drivers of insurance charges.

### 
C. Model Evaluation

To assess model performance, we examined adjusted R-squared values as well as standard regression diagnostic plots.

Model 2 produced a higher adjusted R-squared than Model 1, indicating improved explanatory power after including the interaction between smoking status and BMI. This suggests that allowing BMI to have a different effect for smokers improves model fit.

Residual diagnostic plots were used to evaluate linear regression assumptions. The residuals versus fitted plot did not show a strong pattern, suggesting that the linearity assumption is reasonably satisfied. The scale-location plot indicates relatively stable variance across fitted values, although minor variability remains at higher predicted costs.

The normal Q–Q plot shows that residuals are approximately normally distributed, with some deviation in the tails. This is common in real-world cost data and does not appear severe enough to invalidate the model.

The residuals versus leverage plot shows a few moderate leverage points, but no extreme influential observations that would substantially distort the model.

Overall, the diagnostics suggest that Model 2 reasonably satisfies the key assumptions of linear regression and provides a stable and interpretable predictive framework.

### D. Refinements

The primary refinement made during the modeling process was the addition of an interaction term between smoking status and BMI in Model 2.

Model 1 assumed that the effect of BMI on charges was the same for both smokers and non-smokers. However, exploratory analysis suggested that smokers experience much higher costs overall, and it was reasonable to consider that BMI may have a stronger impact among smokers.

After including the interaction term, Model 2 showed an increase in adjusted R-squared from approximately 0.755 to 0.772. This improvement indicates that allowing BMI to vary by smoking status better captures variation in insurance charges.

In addition, the interaction term was statistically significant, further supporting its inclusion. Diagnostic plots did not show any meaningful deterioration in model assumptions after adding the interaction.

Based on these findings, Model 2 provides a better balance between explanatory power and interpretability compared to Model 1,

## Final Recommendations

Based on the analysis, we recommend using Model 2 as the final predictive model for estimating insurance charges.

Model 2 provides stronger explanatory power than Model 1, as indicated by its higher adjusted R-squared value. The inclusion of the interaction between smoking status and BMI meaningfully improves model fit and captures important differences in how BMI affects smokers versus non-smokers.

The results show that smoking status is the strongest driver of increased insurance costs. Age, BMI, and prior claims history are also significant predictors of higher expected charges. In particular, BMI has a larger impact on expected costs for smokers, which is important for accurately assessing risk.

Overall, Model 2 offers a balance between predictive accuracy and interpretability. It provides the insurance company with a reliable framework for estimating expected charges while clearly identifying the most important cost drivers. This model can support pricing decisions, risk assessment, and policy evaluation moving forward

## Author Contributions

Varun Gullanki was responsible for data cleaning, model construction, regression diagnostics, and refinement of the interaction model.

Amal Parekh was reposonsible for exploratory data analysis, interpretation of model results, and organization and formatting of the final report.

Both authors collaborated on model evaluation, interpretation of findings, and development of the final recommendations.

## Code Appendix

```{r}
library(tidyverse)

ins <- read_csv("insurance_data_S26.csv")

glimpse(ins)
summary(ins)

ggplot(ins, aes(charges)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40)
ggplot(ins, aes(log(charges))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40)

ins <- ins %>%
  mutate(
    sex = factor(sex),
    smoker = factor(smoker),
    region = factor(region),
    month = factor(month)
  )

ggplot(ins, aes(smoker, charges)) +
  geom_boxplot()

m1 <- lm(log(charges) ~ age + sex + bmi + children + smoker + region + month + priorclaims,
         data = ins)

summary(m1)

ggplot(ins, aes(smoker, log(charges))) +
  geom_boxplot()

m2 <- lm(log(charges) ~ age + sex + bmi + children + smoker +
           region + month + priorclaims +
           smoker:bmi,
         data = ins)

summary(m2)

par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plot(m2)
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
```
